---
title: "Web Scraping and Data Collection"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

## Web Scraping Fundamentals {.section-divider}

## What Is Web Scraping?

Getting data from websites is a task for which [AI + coding]{.amber} can really increase efficiency.

Three levels of difficulty:

1. **Easy**: extract a table from a static HTML page
2. **Medium**: construct URLs and loop over downloads
3. **Hard**: pages rendered dynamically with JavaScript

## Example 1: Extracting a Table from Wikipedia

This [Wikipedia page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_S%26P_500_companies) maintains a list of all S&P 500 companies with ticker, sector, headquarters, etc.

```python
import pandas as pd

url = "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_S%26P_500_companies"
tables = pd.read_html(url)
sp500 = tables[0]
sp500.to_excel("sp500.xlsx", index=False)
```

Compared to manually typing 500 rows, this takes [seconds]{.amber}.

## How Does `read_html` Work?

Right-click on the Wikipedia page → "View page source" to see the raw HTML. The `read_html` function searches for `<table>` tags, then interprets:

- `<tbody>` = table body
- `<tr>` = table row
- `<th>` = table header
- `<td>` = table data

It builds a pandas DataFrame from the HTML structure --- no AI required, just pattern matching.

## Example 2: FINRA Short Interest Data

[FINRA](https://www.finra.org/finra-data/browse-catalog/equity-short-interest/files) publishes short interest data twice monthly. Clicking a date downloads a CSV. What's the URL pattern?

Right-click → "View page source" → search for a date like `20250430`:

```
https://cdn.finra.org/equity/otcmarket/biweekly/shrt20250430.csv
```

Now we can [automate the download]{.amber} by looping over dates.

## Automating FINRA Downloads

```python
import pandas as pd
from datetime import date, timedelta

frames = []
d = date(2024, 1, 1)
while d <= date(2025, 4, 30):
    url = f"https://cdn.finra.org/equity/otcmarket/biweekly/shrt{d:%Y%m%d}.csv"
    try:
        frames.append(pd.read_csv(url, sep="|"))
    except:
        pass  # not a reporting date --- skip it
    d += timedelta(days=1)

df = pd.concat(frames)
df.to_csv("finra_short_interest.csv", index=False)
```

Try every possible date in a `try-except` block. Only reporting dates (15th and last business day of each month) will succeed.

## The Hard Case: JavaScript-Rendered Pages

Many pages are [dynamically generated]{.amber} with JavaScript. The data we want isn't in the HTML source --- it's fetched by executing JavaScript code.

**Example**: Open a [Tesla 10-K on EDGAR](https://www.sec.gov/ix?doc=/Archives/edgar/data/1318605/000110465925042659/tm252787d2_10ka.htm). The first table lists Tesla's directors. But if you "View page source" and search for "Elon Musk" --- [0 results]{.amber}.

The entire page is populated by JavaScript with the `<script language="JavaScript">` tag.

## Playwright: Automating a Real Browser

[Playwright](https://playwright.dev/) is a library that controls a real browser (Chrome, Firefox, or Safari) from Python or JavaScript. It executes the JavaScript on the page, then lets you read the [rendered]{.amber} HTML.

```python
from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright

with sync_playwright() as p:
    browser = p.chromium.launch()
    page = browser.new_page()
    page.goto("https://www.sec.gov/ix?doc=/Archives/edgar/data/...")

    # Now the JavaScript has executed --- we can see the data
    content = page.content()          # full rendered HTML
    page.screenshot(path="page.png")  # visual snapshot
    browser.close()
```

Unlike `requests` or `read_html`, Playwright sees the page [as a human would]{.amber}.

## Playwright: Key Capabilities

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Navigation & Interaction**]{.card-title}

- Click buttons, fill forms, select dropdowns
- Wait for elements to load
- Handle login pages and cookies
- Download files triggered by clicks
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Data Extraction**]{.card-title}

- Read rendered HTML after JavaScript executes
- Take screenshots of pages or elements
- Extract text, tables, links
- Record and replay browser sessions
:::
:::

Install: `pip install playwright && playwright install`

## Playwright MCP: AI-Controlled Browsing

The [Playwright MCP server](https://github.com/anthropics/mcp-playwright) connects Playwright to Claude Code, letting the AI [control the browser directly]{.amber}.

```json
// ~/.claude/.mcp.json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@playwright/mcp@latest"]
    }
  }
}
```

Once configured, Claude Code can navigate pages, click links, fill forms, take screenshots, and extract data --- all through natural language instructions.

## When to Use Each Tool

| Scenario | Tool | Why |
|----------|------|-----|
| Static HTML table | `pd.read_html` | Simplest, fastest |
| File download by URL pattern | `requests` / `curl` | Construct URLs, loop |
| API with JSON responses | `requests` | Parse structured data |
| JavaScript-rendered page | **Playwright** | Executes JS, sees rendered page |
| Complex multi-step scraping | **Playwright MCP** | AI navigates and decides |
| Login-protected content | **Playwright** or **MCP** | Handles authentication flows |

For our Magnolia project, we used the EDGAR [API]{.amber} and [direct downloads]{.amber} where possible, avoiding the need for browser automation.

## Case Study: Collecting Financial Documents {.section-divider}

## Goal: Company Research Library

We want to collect a comprehensive set of financial documents for a single company --- the kind of library an analyst would build before a valuation.

**Target company**: [Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY)]{.amber} --- a Houston-based small-cap E&P company operating in the Eagle Ford Shale.

**What we need**:

- 10-K annual reports (MD&A, risk factors, financial statements)
- Earnings call transcripts and press releases
- News articles

## Why Magnolia?

- **Public and small-cap** --- filings are freely available but analyst coverage is thinner
- **Houston-headquartered** --- relevant to Rice students
- **Simple business model** --- onshore oil & gas E&P, easy to understand financially
- **7 years of public history** (IPO 2018) --- enough depth for trend analysis

## Data Sources Overview

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 16
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '22px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 60, 'rankSpacing': 80, 'padding': 16, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart LR
  EDGAR["<b>SEC EDGAR</b><br>10-Ks, 8-Ks"] --> CORPUS["<b>Document Library</b><br>48+ documents"]
  MF["<b>Motley Fool</b><br>Transcripts"] --> CORPUS
  IR["<b>Company IR</b><br>Press releases"] --> CORPUS
  NEWS["<b>News Sites</b><br>Articles"] --> CORPUS

  style EDGAR fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
  style MF fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
  style IR fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
  style NEWS fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
  style CORPUS fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
```

## Step 1: Finding the 10-Ks {.section-divider}

## The EDGAR API

SEC EDGAR provides a free REST API that returns filing metadata as JSON. No API key required --- just a `User-Agent` header.

```python
import requests, json

url = "https://data.sec.gov/submissions/CIK0001698990.json"
headers = {"User-Agent": "Kerry Back keb7@rice.edu"}
data = requests.get(url, headers=headers).json()

for i, form in enumerate(data["filings"]["recent"]["form"]):
    if form == "10-K":
        date = data["filings"]["recent"]["filingDate"][i]
        doc = data["filings"]["recent"]["primaryDocument"][i]
        print(f"{date}: {doc}")
```

## What We Got: 7 Years of 10-Ks

| Fiscal Year | Filing Date | Document |
|-------------|-------------|----------|
| 2025 | 2026-02-12 | `mgy-20251231.htm` |
| 2024 | 2025-02-19 | `mgy-20241231.htm` |
| 2023 | 2024-02-15 | `mgy-20231231.htm` |
| 2022 | 2023-02-16 | `mgy-20221231.htm` |
| 2021 | 2022-02-17 | `mgy-20211231.htm` |
| 2020 | 2021-02-23 | `mgy-20201231.htm` |
| 2019 | 2020-02-26 | `a10-k2019.htm` |

All downloaded as HTML directly from EDGAR.

## Step 2: Extracting Sections {.section-divider}

## Parsing 10-K HTML with Python

The raw EDGAR HTML files are cluttered with CSS, XBRL tags, and formatting. We used [BeautifulSoup]{.amber} to extract three sections from each 10-K:

```python
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

with open("mgy-10k-2025.htm") as f:
    soup = BeautifulSoup(f, "html.parser")

# Find section headers like "Item 7" or "Item 1A"
# Extract text between section boundaries
# Convert HTML tables to markdown pipe format
# Strip XBRL tags and embedded styles
```

## 21 Extracted Files

For each of the 7 fiscal years, we extracted:

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**MD&A**]{.card-title}

- Management's Discussion & Analysis (Item 7)
- Revenue drivers, cost trends, liquidity
- 33--46 KB per year
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Risk Factors**]{.card-title}

- Item 1A: all risk categories
- Commodity prices, regulatory, operational
- 70--83 KB per year
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Financial Statements**]{.card-title}

- Balance sheets, income statements, cash flows
- Equity statements + all footnotes
- 120--174 KB per year
:::
:::

## Example: Balance Sheet (FY2025)

```
| ASSETS                                  | Dec 31, 2025 | Dec 31, 2024 |
|-----------------------------------------|--------------|--------------|
| Cash and cash equivalents               | $266,785     | $260,049     |
| Total current assets                    | 442,435      | 410,824      |
| Oil and natural gas properties          | 4,957,292    | 4,403,210    |
| Accumulated DD&A                        | (2,553,758)  | (2,115,892)  |
| Total property, plant & equipment, net  | 2,424,152    | 2,306,034    |
| TOTAL ASSETS                            | $2,903,092   | $2,820,835   |
```

*(In thousands. Extracted from [mgy-2025-financial-statements.md](../magnolia/10k/extracted/mgy-2025-financial-statements.md))*

## Example: Income Statement (FY2025)

```
| REVENUES                | 2025        | 2024        | 2023        |
|-------------------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Oil revenues            | $918,027    | $1,046,675  | $958,388    |
| Natural gas revenues    | 190,252     | 90,277      | 102,054     |
| NGL revenues            | 203,566     | 178,934     | 166,537     |
| Total revenues          | 1,311,845   | 1,315,886   | 1,226,979   |
| OPERATING INCOME        | 439,181     | 511,988     | 534,485     |
| NET INCOME              | 337,279     | 397,330     | 442,604     |
| EPS (diluted)           | $1.73       | $1.94       | $2.04       |
```

*(In thousands except per share. From [mgy-2025-financial-statements.md](../magnolia/10k/extracted/mgy-2025-financial-statements.md))*

## Step 3: Earnings Calls {.section-divider}

## Two Sources of Earnings Data

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 16
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '22px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 60, 'rankSpacing': 80, 'padding': 16, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart TD
  EC["<b>Quarterly Earnings</b><br>28 quarters (Q2 2019 -- Q4 2025)"]
  EC --> T["<b>Full Transcripts</b><br>6 quarters<br>(Motley Fool)"]
  EC --> PR["<b>Press Releases</b><br>21 quarters<br>(EDGAR 8-K filings)"]

  style EC fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
  style T fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
  style PR fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:14px
```

- **Transcripts** include management commentary and analyst Q&A
- **Press releases** include financial tables, production data, and guidance
- Together they cover [every quarter]{.amber} of Magnolia's public history

## Transcript Sources: The Challenge

Full earnings call transcripts are hard to get for free:

| Source | Coverage | Access |
|--------|----------|--------|
| Motley Fool | Spotty for small-caps | Free |
| Seeking Alpha | Complete | Paywalled |
| MarketScreener | Complete | Paywalled |
| GuruFocus | Complete | JS-rendered / paywalled |
| Company webcasts | Audio only | Registration wall |

**Our approach**: Grab what's free from Motley Fool, fill gaps with EDGAR press releases.

## Fetching Transcripts from Motley Fool

```python
# WebFetch extracts the full transcript from Motley Fool
# Example: Q3 2025 earnings call
url = ("https://www.fool.com/earnings/call-transcripts/"
       "2025/10/30/magnolia-oil-and-gas-mgy-earnings-transcript/")
```

**What we captured**: prepared remarks, financial highlights, management guidance, and the full analyst Q&A.

Example output: [mgy-q3-2025-earnings.md](../magnolia/transcripts/mgy-q3-2025-earnings.md)

## Fetching Press Releases from EDGAR

For quarters without free transcripts, we downloaded [Exhibit 99.1]{.amber} from each earnings 8-K filing:

```python
# Same EDGAR API --- filter for 8-K filings
# Download the Exhibit 99.1 (earnings press release)
# Contains: financial tables, production data, guidance

url = ("https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1698990/"
       "000169899025000028/ex991-3q2025earningsrele.htm")
```

Example output: [mgy-q4-2024-press-release.md](../magnolia/transcripts/mgy-q4-2024-press-release.md)

## Example: Transcript Excerpt (Q3 2025)

> **Christopher Stavros, CEO**: "We have a lot of both financial and operational flexibility, especially considering some of those deferrals that have snaked through the system in 2025."

Key data points extracted:

- Record production: [100,500 BOE/day]{.amber} (11% YoY growth)
- Free cash flow: $134 million
- Adjusted EBITDAX: $219 million
- Dividend increased 15% to $0.15/share quarterly

## Step 4: News Articles {.section-divider}

## Scraping Financial News

The final layer of the corpus: news articles from financial media covering Magnolia over the past 2--3 years.

**Target sources**: Reuters, Yahoo Finance, Hart Energy, Houston Chronicle, Rigzone

**What we're looking for**:

- Earnings coverage and analyst reactions
- Acquisition announcements
- Production and guidance updates
- Oil price impact analysis
- Dividend and buyback news

## The Complete Corpus {.section-divider}

## What We Built

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**10-K Extractions**]{.card-title}

- 7 fiscal years (2019--2025)
- 21 files: MD&A + Risk Factors + Financials
- ~900 KB of clean markdown
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Earnings Data**]{.card-title}

- 6 full transcripts
- 21 press releases
- 27 files covering every quarter
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**News Articles**]{.card-title}

- Financial media coverage
- Analyst commentary
- Industry context
:::
:::

All stored in `magnolia/` as clean, structured text.

## Corpus File Structure

```
magnolia/
├── 10k/
│   ├── mgy-10k-2019.htm ... mgy-10k-2025.htm    (raw EDGAR HTML)
│   └── extracted/
│       ├── mgy-2019-mda.md ... mgy-2025-mda.md
│       ├── mgy-2019-risk-factors.md ... mgy-2025-risk-factors.md
│       └── mgy-2019-financial-statements.md ... mgy-2025-financial-statements.md
├── transcripts/
│   ├── mgy-q2-2019-earnings.md ... mgy-q3-2025-earnings.md    (6 transcripts)
│   └── mgy-q2-2020-press-release.md ... mgy-q4-2025-press-release.md  (21 PRs)
└── news/
    └── (articles)
```

## Key Techniques Summary

| Step | Tool / API | What It Does |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| Find filings | EDGAR Submissions API | Returns filing metadata as JSON |
| Download 10-Ks | `curl` + EDGAR URLs | Fetches HTML filings directly |
| Parse HTML | BeautifulSoup | Strips XBRL/CSS, extracts sections |
| Convert tables | Python script | HTML tables → markdown pipe format |
| Get transcripts | WebFetch + Motley Fool | Extracts full call transcripts |
| Get press releases | EDGAR 8-K Exhibit 99.1 | Quarterly financial data |
| Scrape news | WebSearch + WebFetch | Articles from financial media |

## Lessons Learned

**What worked well**:

- EDGAR API is [reliable and free]{.amber} --- no authentication needed
- Constructing URLs from patterns (like the FINRA example) scales to many filings
- `pd.read_html` and BeautifulSoup handle static HTML tables well
- Motley Fool provides full transcripts for free (when they cover the company)

**What was hard**:

- Transcript coverage for small-caps is [spotty]{.amber} on free sources
- Most transcript providers (Seeking Alpha, MarketScreener) are paywalled
- Company webcasts use JS-based players (the same JavaScript problem we saw with Tesla/EDGAR)
- EDGAR HTML is messy --- XBRL tags and inline styles require serious parsing

## Summary

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 16
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '20px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 50, 'rankSpacing': 70, 'padding': 14, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart LR
  S1["<b>1. Identify<br>Sources</b>"] --> S2["<b>2. Download<br>Documents</b>"]
  S2 --> S3["<b>3. Parse &<br>Extract</b>"]
  S3 --> S4["<b>4. Collect<br>Transcripts</b>"]
  S4 --> S5["<b>5. Scrape<br>News</b>"]
  S5 --> S6["<b>6. Clean &<br>Structure</b>"]

  style S1 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:12px
  style S2 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:12px
  style S3 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:12px
  style S4 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:12px
  style S5 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:12px
  style S6 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:20px,padding:12px
```

Web scraping is fundamentally a [data engineering]{.amber} problem: finding sources, downloading documents, parsing messy formats, and converting everything to clean, structured text that AI tools can work with.
